name: Logz.io
what_it_does: AI-powered SaaS log management and observability platform built on a managed ELK Stack, providing log ingestion, search, ML-based alerting, and anomaly detection via REST API.
use_cases: Querying application logs programmatically to diagnose production incidents in agent-driven SRE workflows
Creating and managing alert rules via API for automated monitoring pipeline setup
Shipping logs from agents and pipelines using the logzio-python-handler or Filebeat/Logstash integrations
Automated anomaly detection and alert correlation for security monitoring and SIEM use cases
not_for: Teams needing metrics and tracing as a primary use case — Logz.io's strength is logs; metrics and tracing are secondary products
Organizations that need to self-host their observability stack for data sovereignty
Very high-volume log environments where per-GB pricing makes costs prohibitive compared to self-managed ELK
best_when: Best when a team wants a fully managed ELK experience with ML-powered alerting and no Elasticsearch operational overhead, and needs a REST API for programmatic alert management and log querying.
avoid_when: Avoid when you need a unified metrics-traces-logs platform (use Grafana Cloud or Datadog instead), or when data residency requirements conflict with Logz.io's regional offerings.

auth.notes: Each Logz.io account type (Logs, Metrics, Tracing, SIEM) has its own API token. Tokens are account-scoped and do not support fine-grained permission scoping. Log shipping uses a separate 'Shipping Token' distinct from the API token.

pricing.free_tier_limits: 1 GB/day log ingestion with 3-day retention. Community tier available for individuals.
pricing.paid_tiers: Pro plans start around $0.74/GB ingested with longer retention options (7, 14, 30, 60 days). Volume discounts available. Annual contracts reduce per-GB cost.
pricing.notes: Pricing is primarily based on daily ingestion volume. Retention length significantly affects price. SIEM (Cloud SIEM product) is priced separately from log management.

performance.rate_limits: API rate limits apply per account and are not publicly specified in exact numbers. Log shipping endpoints are designed for high throughput. Search API queries against large datasets may have longer response times due to underlying Elasticsearch query complexity.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: API errors return JSON with message fields, but HTTP 400 errors from the search API can reflect underlying Elasticsearch query syntax errors that require Elasticsearch knowledge to interpret. Alert API errors are clearer.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Alert creation via API is not idempotent — submitting the same alert definition twice creates duplicate alerts. Agents must check for existing alerts before creating new ones.
known_agent_gotchas: Separate API tokens for Logs, Metrics, Tracing, and SIEM accounts — agents must manage multiple credentials
Search API uses Elasticsearch DSL query syntax, not a simplified abstraction — agents need to construct valid Elasticsearch queries
Log shipping token and API management token are different — confusion between them is a common integration failure
Kibana Discover filters and saved searches cannot be directly queried via API without translating to ES DSL
Account ID (found in account settings) is required in API paths and not discoverable via API itself

security_score_components.security_notes: API tokens are account-wide with no scope granularity, which is a meaningful risk for agent use cases where least-privilege access is preferred. HIPAA BAA available on qualifying plans. SOC2 Type II certified.
